headline: Government Raya Open Houses Cancelled: Modest Savings, Real Trade-offs
context: Cabinet ordered the cancellation of Hari Raya open houses by government agencies, GLCs and GLICs, and restricted ministers' overseas travel as a cost-moderation signal. Reported lost private-sector revenue from these cancellations was estimated at RM15-25M; MOF said petrol and diesel subsidies could reach RM4B per month.

cards.big: Government open houses are off this Raya. The savings sit alongside fuel-subsidy figures roughly two orders of magnitude larger.
cards.sub: The order covers government agencies, GLCs and GLICs, not all open houses in Malaysia. Private and household events are not in scope.

cards.big: Hari Raya open houses are an established part of Malaysia's official calendar and are described in policy as fostering inter-community ties.
cards.sub: Official open houses are taxpayer-funded; one documented example was RM2.88M for the 2013 Prime Minister and Cabinet open house. Scale and procurement vary by year and host.

cards.big: Reported lost private-sector revenue from cancelled official events was estimated at RM15-25M across ministries and agencies.
cards.sub: Catering can run 50-60% of event budgets. DOSM records women-owned establishments in F&B, though we did not verify a female-dominated ownership pattern in catering specifically.

cards.big: Other Muslim-majority states scaled down festive hospitality rather than eliminating official events. Malaysia chose a full ban for the sectors named.
cards.sub: The PMO framed it as spending moderation. Whether that signal lands depends on whether other government cost lines move proportionally.

cards.big: The question is not whether to host this year. It is whether procurement for official open houses is transparent enough to scale them without scrapping them.
Symbolic austerity is real signalling, but small in fiscal terms. A durable answer is published procurement and audited costs for official festive events, not an on-off switch.
